---
title: "The Asymptotic Trinity: Likelihood-Ratio, Wald, and Score Tests"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20241007)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # data / point estimates / the log-likelihood curve
CURVE  = "#c1121f"     # theoretical limit / reference curves
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # the MLE / the truth / highlights
LR     = "#7b2cbf"     # likelihood-ratio flavour
WALD   = "#0a7d4d"     # Wald flavour
SCORE  = "#d6791f"     # score flavour
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas / histograms
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the capstone of the **Hypothesis Testing**
> movement (D). It assumes [INFR-B2 *Maximum Likelihood*] (the MLE
> $\hat\theta_n=\arg\max_\theta\ell_n(\theta)$ and the **score**
> $U_n(\theta)=\ell_n'(\theta)$ whose root we chase); [INFR-B3 *Fisher Information*] (the
> per-observation information $I_1(\theta)$ as the variance of the score and as the
> negative expected curvature, with total information $I_n=nI_1$); [INFR-B4 *MLE
> Asymptotics*], whose two headline facts — $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}
> \mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1})$ and the score's CLT
> $\frac1{\sqrt n}U_n(\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0))$ — are the entire
> engine of this article; [INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*] (null vs.
> alternative, Type I/II error, the level $\alpha$, power, and the p-value as a tail
> probability under the null); and [INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*] for the
> **chi-square distribution** as the law of a sum of squared standard Normals. It is the
> test-side companion of [INFR-C3 *Wald, Score, and Profile Intervals*]: the three intervals
> there and the three tests here are the same three objects, read as a confidence set or as
> a decision. Nothing new is imported from outside the track. It **sets up** the asymptotic
> inference the entire **Regression** track relies on — every deviance test for nested GLMs,
> every coefficient $z$-test, every score (Lagrange-multiplier) diagnostic is one of these
> three.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

[INFR-D2] gave us a test for each everyday question — a $z$ for a mean, a $\chi^2$ for a
contingency table, an $F$ for nested linear models — but each was built by hand for its
setting. This article supplies the *general* machinery: three test statistics that test
**any** hypothesis about **any** model fit by maximum likelihood, and the single theorem
that gives all three the same null distribution. The **likelihood-ratio (LR)** statistic
compares the height of the log-likelihood at its unconstrained peak to its height under
the null; **Wilks' theorem** proves that twice that drop converges to a chi-square whose
degrees of freedom equal the number of constraints the null imposes — and we derive it,
not assert it. The **Wald** statistic measures, in standard-error units, how far the
unconstrained estimate sits from the null value. The **score (Rao / Lagrange-multiplier)**
statistic measures how steeply the log-likelihood is still climbing *at* the null value.
We show all three are the same quadratic read three ways — horizontal distance, vertical
drop, and slope on the log-likelihood curve — prove their asymptotic equivalence, and
explain exactly when and why they disagree for finite $n$. We then prove the
**Neyman–Pearson lemma**: for testing one simple hypothesis against another, the
likelihood-ratio test is the *most powerful* test at its level — the theorem that explains
why likelihood ratios sit at the center of all three. The single claim the article defends
is that **the trinity is not three rival recipes but one geometry — the curvature of the
log-likelihood at and around its peak — and that knowing which corner of that geometry a
test reads tells you exactly when to trust it.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
To test a null hypothesis $H_0$ about a model fit by maximum likelihood, read the
log-likelihood curve $\ell_n(\theta)$ three ways. Let $\hat\theta$ be the unconstrained
MLE and $\theta_0$ the value the null asserts.

1. **Likelihood-ratio** — how far the curve **drops** from its free peak to the null:
   $$
   \Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}} = 2\bigl[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)\bigr].
   $$
2. **Wald** — how far the **estimate** sits from the null, in squared standard-error units
   (curvature measured *at the peak*):
   $$
   \Lambda_{\mathrm{W}} = (\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2\,I_n(\hat\theta).
   $$
3. **Score (Rao)** — how steeply the curve is still **climbing** at the null value (slope
   and curvature measured *at $\theta_0$*):
   $$
   \Lambda_{\mathrm{S}} = \frac{U_n(\theta_0)^2}{I_n(\theta_0)}.
   $$

**Wilks' theorem** gives all three the same large-sample null distribution: each converges
to $\chi^2_q$, where $q$ is the number of free parameters the null pins down. They are
**equal as $n\to\infty$** and **disagree exactly when the log-likelihood is not a
symmetric parabola**. And the **Neyman–Pearson lemma** explains why the likelihood ratio
is the object at the center: for a simple-vs-simple test it is the *most powerful* test at
its level. The single picture: **LR reads the vertical drop, Wald the horizontal distance,
score the slope — three coordinates of one curvature.**
:::

Everything below orbits that box. Each statistic measures the same thing — "how
incompatible is the null with the likelihood?" — through a different feature of one curve:
its *height* (LR), the *location of its peak* (Wald), or its *slope at the null* (score).
On a perfect parabola the three coincide; the entire practical story of the trinity is
*where the curve is not a parabola*, which is exactly where they part ways and exactly
where one of them is right and the others lie.

# Why we are doing this: from a test per problem to a test for everything {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **we can fit any model by maximum
likelihood — how do we test a hypothesis about it without inventing a new test from
scratch each time?** Every derivation below is an answer to a piece of that.

## The problem the workhorse tests left open

[INFR-D2] is a catalog. The one-sample $z$-test handles a Normal mean with known variance;
the $t$-test handles it with unknown variance; Pearson's $\chi^2$ handles a multinomial
goodness-of-fit; the $F$-test handles nested *linear* models. Each statistic was
hand-built: a pivot was found, its null distribution was worked out, and a rejection
region was drawn. That is fine when a clean pivot exists. But the moment we leave the
comfortable Normal-and-linear world — logistic regression, Poisson counts, a Weibull
survival model, a mixture, *any* model whose likelihood we can write but whose estimator
has no closed-form sampling distribution — the catalog runs out. There is no "$t$-test for
whether a logistic-regression coefficient is zero" in the sense of an exact pivot; there
is only the likelihood.

So we need a testing principle as general as maximum likelihood itself: given *any*
log-likelihood $\ell_n(\theta)$ and *any* hypothesis $H_0$, produce a statistic and a null
distribution. That is precisely what the trinity delivers — and the reason all three share
one null distribution is the same asymptotic-normality engine that [INFR-B4] already built.
This article is, in a real sense, [INFR-B4] turned from estimation into testing.

## Why three, and not one

A natural first reaction: if all three are asymptotically equivalent, why carry three? The
honest answer — the one a sharp examiner is after — is **computational cost and
finite-sample behavior**, and they pull in opposite directions.

- The **Wald** statistic needs only the *unconstrained* fit $\hat\theta$ and the curvature
  there. You fit the full model once; the null value never enters the optimization. This is
  why every regression table prints Wald $z$-statistics: they are free once the model is fit.
- The **score** statistic needs only the fit *under the null* $\tilde\theta$. You fit the
  restricted (often much simpler) model and ask whether the data are pulling away from it.
  This is why the score test is the natural **diagnostic** — "should I add this term?" —
  testable without ever fitting the larger model.
- The **likelihood-ratio** statistic needs *both* fits, the constrained and the
  unconstrained. It is the most work but, as we will see, usually the most trustworthy when
  the likelihood is skewed.

So the choice is driven by *which fit you already have in hand* and *how trustworthy the
quadratic approximation is for your sample*. Keeping all three is not redundancy; it is
having the right tool for whichever fit is cheap and whichever approximation is safe.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the three tests are asymptotically equivalent, does it ever matter which one I use?"*
Yes — for two reasons an interviewer wants you to separate. **Computationally**, they
require different fits (Wald: full model only; score: null model only; LR: both), so one
may be far cheaper in a given workflow. **Statistically**, they are equivalent only *in the
limit*; for finite $n$ they can give materially different p-values, and they disagree most
exactly when it matters most — skewed likelihoods, small samples, parameters near a
boundary. The standard guidance: the LR test is generally the most reliable (it is
invariant to reparameterization and uses the curve's actual shape), the Wald test is the
least reliable when the likelihood is skewed (it can even be *non-monotone* in the data —
the Hauck–Donner effect), and the score test is the safe choice when you cannot or will not
fit the full model. "Asymptotically equivalent" is a promise about $n\to\infty$, not a
license to treat them as interchangeable at your $n$.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

One picture carries the whole article: the **log-likelihood curve**, with its peak at the
MLE $\hat\theta$ and the null value $\theta_0$ marked on the axis. Near its peak a smooth
log-likelihood looks like a downward parabola (this is [INFR-B4]'s Taylor expansion, and we
re-derive it in @sec-quadratic), and a parabola is pinned down by three numbers: where its
top is, how tall it is, and how steeply its sides fall. The three tests read exactly these
three features.

```{python}
#| label: fig-trinity-geometry
#| fig-cap: 'The trinity as three readings of one curve. The black curve is a log-likelihood $\ell_n(\theta)$, peaked at the MLE $\hat\theta$ (green). The null asserts $\theta=\theta_0$ (red tick). **Wald** reads the *horizontal* gap $\hat\theta-\theta_0$ and converts it to a height using the curvature at the peak. **Likelihood-ratio** reads the *vertical* drop $\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)$. **Score** reads the *slope* of the curve at $\theta_0$ (orange tangent) — how hard the likelihood is still pulling away from the null. On a parabola these three numbers are algebraically locked together; the entire finite-sample story is how they come apart when the curve is skewed.'
# A deliberately mildly-skewed log-likelihood to show the three readings distinctly.
theta = np.linspace(0.2, 4.2, 400)
# Use a gamma-type log-likelihood shape: peaks near 2, gently skewed
def ell(t):
    return 30*(np.log(t) - t/2.0)   # maximized at t=2, concave, slightly skewed
peak_t = 2.0
ell_peak = ell(peak_t)
theta0 = 1.05

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.4))
ax.plot(theta, ell(theta), color="black", lw=2.2, label=r"$\ell_n(\theta)$")
# peak / MLE
ax.scatter([peak_t], [ell_peak], color=ACCENT, s=60, zorder=6)
ax.axvline(peak_t, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, ls=":")
ax.text(peak_t+0.05, ell(theta).min()+1.0, r"$\hat\theta$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
# null value
ax.axvline(theta0, color=CURVE, lw=1.0, ls=":")
ax.text(theta0-0.30, ell(theta).min()+1.0, r"$\theta_0$", color=CURVE, fontsize=12)
# LR: vertical drop
ax.annotate("", xy=(theta0, ell(theta0)), xytext=(theta0, ell_peak),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=LR, lw=2.0))
ax.text(theta0+0.06, (ell(theta0)+ell_peak)/2, "LR:\nvertical drop", color=LR, fontsize=9)
# Wald: horizontal distance
ax.annotate("", xy=(theta0, ell_peak), xytext=(peak_t, ell_peak),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=WALD, lw=2.0))
ax.text((theta0+peak_t)/2-0.35, ell_peak+0.6, "Wald: horizontal gap", color=WALD, fontsize=9)
# Score: tangent slope at theta0
slope = 30*(1/theta0 - 0.5)
tx = np.linspace(theta0-0.45, theta0+0.55, 50)
ax.plot(tx, ell(theta0) + slope*(tx-theta0), color=SCORE, lw=2.0, ls="--")
ax.text(theta0-0.10, ell(theta0)-3.2, "score:\nslope at $\\theta_0$", color=SCORE, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\theta$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\ell_n(\theta)$")
ax.set_title("Three coordinates of one log-likelihood curve")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_ylim(ell(theta).min()-1, ell_peak+4)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The mental model to hold for the rest of the article: **if the curve were an exact
downward parabola, the horizontal gap, the vertical drop, and the slope would each
determine the other two by simple algebra — they would be one number in three costumes.**
They are. The asymptotic theory says the curve *becomes* a parabola as $n\to\infty$, so the
three become identical. For finite $n$ the curve has some skew, and then the three readings
genuinely differ — Wald trusts the curvature at the peak (which can be a poor guide far
from it), the score trusts the curvature at $\theta_0$, and LR uses the honest height drop.
That single observation predicts every disagreement we will see.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Setup and the hypotheses we test {#sec-setup}

We observe $X_1,\dots,X_n$ i.i.d. from $f(x\mid\theta)$, with log-likelihood and score
$$
\ell_n(\theta)=\sum_{i=1}^n\log f(x_i\mid\theta),
\qquad
U_n(\theta)=\ell_n'(\theta),
$$ {#eq-ell-score}
exactly as in [INFR-B2]. We take the **scalar case** $\theta\in\mathbb R$ for the main
derivations — it carries all the ideas and keeps the algebra visible — and state the
vector generalization (gradients, the information *matrix*, and degrees of freedom equal to
the number of restrictions) where it is the only change. The MLE $\hat\theta_n$ solves
$U_n(\hat\theta_n)=0$, and the total Fisher information is $I_n(\theta)=nI_1(\theta)$
([INFR-B3]).

The hypotheses are about $\theta$. In the scalar case the most common is the **simple
null** against a **two-sided alternative**,
$$
H_0:\ \theta=\theta_0
\qquad\text{vs.}\qquad
H_1:\ \theta\ne\theta_0 ,
$$ {#eq-hypo}
where $\theta_0$ is a fixed value the analyst names (e.g. "the coefficient is zero," so
$\theta_0=0$). When $\theta$ is a vector $(\psi,\lambda)$ split into a parameter of
interest $\psi\in\mathbb R^q$ and a **nuisance** parameter $\lambda$, the null
$H_0:\psi=\psi_0$ imposes $q$ constraints and leaves $\lambda$ free; that $q$ is the
degrees of freedom in Wilks' theorem, and we keep it in view because it is the single
number people most often get wrong.

The two facts from [INFR-B4] we will use relentlessly, recalled here so the article is
self-contained, both holding under that article's regularity conditions and evaluated at
the true $\theta_0$:

::: {#thm-imports}
**Imported facts (from [INFR-B4]).** Under the regularity conditions, as $n\to\infty$:

1. **(Score CLT.)** The score at the truth, normalized, is asymptotically Gaussian with
   variance the information:
   $$
   \frac{1}{\sqrt n}\,U_n(\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\,I_1(\theta_0)\bigr).
   $$
2. **(MLE asymptotic normality.)** The estimation error, magnified by $\sqrt n$, is
   Gaussian:
   $$
   \sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N\!\bigl(0,\,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}\bigr).
   $$
3. **(Curvature converges to information.)** The normalized observed curvature settles onto
   the information: $-\frac1n\ell_n''(\theta_n^\ast)\xrightarrow{P}I_1(\theta_0)$ for any
   $\theta_n^\ast\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0$.
:::

These are not re-proved here — they are the whole content of [INFR-B4], built there with
the Taylor-plus-CLT argument every step shown. What is *new* in this article is turning
them into three test statistics and establishing that each has a chi-square null
distribution.

We also need one fact from [INFR-D2], which we use by name:

::: {.callout-note title="Imported: the chi-square distribution (from INFR-D2)"}
If $Z_1,\dots,Z_q$ are independent standard Normal random variables, then
$\sum_{j=1}^q Z_j^2$ has the **chi-square distribution with $q$ degrees of freedom**,
written $\chi^2_q$. In particular the **square of a single standard Normal is $\chi^2_1$** —
the fact that turns each of our (asymptotically Normal) statistics, once squared, into a
chi-square. [INFR-D2] builds this distribution from scratch as the law of a sum of squared
Normals; we consume it.
:::

## The quadratic approximation: one curve, three coordinates {#sec-quadratic}

The trinity's unity comes from a single Taylor expansion. We expand the log-likelihood
about its peak $\hat\theta$ — the same expansion [INFR-B4] used, now pointed at testing.

Because $\hat\theta$ maximizes $\ell_n$ at an interior point, the first derivative there
vanishes: $U_n(\hat\theta)=\ell_n'(\hat\theta)=0$. Taylor-expand $\ell_n(\theta)$ about
$\hat\theta$ to second order (the Calc-1 Taylor expansion, $g(\theta)\approx
g(\hat\theta)+g'(\hat\theta)(\theta-\hat\theta)+\tfrac12 g''(\hat\theta)(\theta-\hat\theta)^2$),
and use that the linear term drops because $\ell_n'(\hat\theta)=0$:
$$
\ell_n(\theta)\ \approx\ \ell_n(\hat\theta)
+ \underbrace{\ell_n'(\hat\theta)}_{=\,0}(\theta-\hat\theta)
+ \tfrac12\,\ell_n''(\hat\theta)\,(\theta-\hat\theta)^2
= \ell_n(\hat\theta) - \tfrac12\,J_n(\hat\theta)\,(\theta-\hat\theta)^2 ,
$$ {#eq-quad}
where we name the **observed information** $J_n(\theta)=-\ell_n''(\theta)$, the negative
curvature of the log-likelihood. (It is positive at a maximum, since $\ell_n''(\hat\theta)
<0$ there — the curve bends down.) By imported fact 3, $\frac1n J_n(\hat\theta)\to
I_1(\theta_0)$, so $J_n(\hat\theta)\approx I_n(\theta_0)\approx I_n(\hat\theta)$ for large
$n$; we will move between $J_n(\hat\theta)$ and $I_n(\hat\theta)$ freely, flagging it, since
they share the limit.

@eq-quad says the log-likelihood is, near its peak, a **downward parabola** of height
$\ell_n(\hat\theta)$ and curvature $J_n(\hat\theta)$. Everything the three tests measure is
a feature of this parabola, and reading off all three is now pure algebra. Evaluate
@eq-quad at the null value $\theta_0$:
$$
\ell_n(\theta_0)\ \approx\ \ell_n(\hat\theta) - \tfrac12\,J_n(\hat\theta)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 .
$$ {#eq-quad-at-null}

**The LR drop.** Rearranging @eq-quad-at-null,
$$
2\bigl[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)\bigr]\ \approx\ J_n(\hat\theta)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 .
$$ {#eq-LRquad}

**The Wald distance.** The right-hand side of @eq-LRquad, with $J_n(\hat\theta)$ replaced
by its limit-equivalent $I_n(\hat\theta)$, is *exactly the Wald statistic*
$(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 I_n(\hat\theta)$.

**The score slope.** Differentiate @eq-quad once in $\theta$ to get the score near the peak,
$U_n(\theta)=\ell_n'(\theta)\approx -J_n(\hat\theta)(\theta-\hat\theta)$, and evaluate at
$\theta_0$:
$$
U_n(\theta_0)\ \approx\ -J_n(\hat\theta)\,(\theta_0-\hat\theta) = J_n(\hat\theta)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0).
$$
Square it and divide by $I_n$ (again the limit-equivalent of $J_n$):
$$
\frac{U_n(\theta_0)^2}{I_n}\ \approx\ \frac{J_n(\hat\theta)^2(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2}{I_n}
\ \approx\ I_n(\hat\theta)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 .
$$ {#eq-scorequad}

Stand back. @eq-LRquad, the Wald statistic, and @eq-scorequad are **the same quantity**
$I_n(\hat\theta)(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2$, to leading order. That is the algebraic heart of
the trinity: on the quadratic approximation the three statistics are not merely close, they
are *equal*. Their differences are entirely in the higher-order terms the parabola dropped
— which is why they coincide as $n\to\infty$ (the curve becomes parabolic) and diverge when
the curve is skewed (the dropped terms matter). @fig-trinity-geometry was this fact drawn.

::: {.callout-important title="The one identity to carry"}
Near the peak, all three statistics equal
$$
I_n(\hat\theta)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2
= \Bigl(\tfrac{\hat\theta-\theta_0}{\mathrm{SE}}\Bigr)^2,
\qquad \mathrm{SE}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta)}},
$$
the **squared standardized distance** of the estimate from the null. Wald measures it at
the peak's curvature, score at the null's, LR by the honest height drop — three estimates
of one number. When the log-likelihood is a symmetric parabola they agree exactly; the rest
of the article is the catalog of what happens when it is not.
:::

## The Wald test {#sec-wald}

The Wald statistic is imported fact 2 read as a test. By that fact,
$\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1})$ *under the
null* (when $\theta_0$ is the truth). Standardize: divide by the asymptotic standard
deviation $\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}}=1/\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}$, i.e. multiply by
$\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}$,
$$
\sqrt n\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\,\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}
= \sqrt{n I_1(\theta_0)}\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)
= \sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}\,(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N(0,1).
$$
That is the **Wald $z$-statistic** $Z_{\mathrm W}=(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\sqrt{I_n}$.
Squaring a standard Normal gives a $\chi^2_1$ (the imported chi-square fact), so

::: {#thm-wald}
**Theorem (Wald test).** Under $H_0:\theta=\theta_0$ and the regularity conditions,
$$
\Lambda_{\mathrm W} = (\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)^2\,I_n(\hat\theta_n)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \chi^2_1 .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
By imported fact 2, $\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal
N(0,I_1(\theta_0)^{-1})$ under the null. Multiplying a $\mathcal N(0,v)$ variable by the
constant $c$ scales its variance by $c^2$, so multiplying by $\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}$ gives
$\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}\cdot\sqrt n(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal
N\bigl(0,\,I_1(\theta_0)\cdot I_1(\theta_0)^{-1}\bigr)=\mathcal N(0,1)$. The left side is
$\sqrt{n I_1(\theta_0)}(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)=\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)$.
We must replace the unknown $I_n(\theta_0)$ by the computable $I_n(\hat\theta_n)$: since
$I_1$ is continuous and $\hat\theta_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0$ (consistency, [INFR-B4]),
$I_1(\hat\theta_n)\xrightarrow{P}I_1(\theta_0)$, so the ratio
$\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta_n)/I_n(\theta_0)}\xrightarrow{P}1$, and **Slutsky's theorem** lets us
substitute without changing the limit. Hence $\sqrt{I_n(\hat\theta_n)}(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)
\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$. Finally, if $Z\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$ then
$Z^2\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_1$ by the continuous-mapping theorem and the imported chi-square
fact (the square of a standard Normal is $\chi^2_1$). Squaring the standardized statistic
gives exactly $\Lambda_{\mathrm W}=(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)^2 I_n(\hat\theta_n)$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The Wald test's selling point is laid bare in the proof: **it uses only the unconstrained
fit.** You compute $\hat\theta_n$ and the information at it, both byproducts of fitting the
full model, and the null value $\theta_0$ enters only at the very end as a number you
subtract. This is why software prints "$z=\hat\theta/\mathrm{SE}$" for every coefficient —
that is the Wald statistic for $H_0:\theta=0$, and its square is $\Lambda_{\mathrm W}$.

Its weakness is equally visible: it measures curvature *at the peak* $J_n(\hat\theta)$, then
trusts that curvature all the way out to $\theta_0$. If $\theta_0$ is far from $\hat\theta$
and the likelihood is skewed, the curvature at the peak is the wrong scale for the distance
traveled — and the Wald statistic is miscalibrated. We see this fail concretely in
@sec-failure.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The Wald test is not invariant to how you parameterize the model.** Test $H_0:\theta=1$
via $\Lambda_{\mathrm W}=(\hat\theta-1)^2 I_n(\hat\theta)$, or test the algebraically
*identical* hypothesis $H_0:\log\theta=0$ via the Wald statistic for $\log\theta$, and you
get **different p-values** — because the Wald statistic depends on the *shape* of the
parameterization through the second derivative, and $\theta\mapsto\log\theta$ bends the
curve. The likelihood-ratio statistic, which reads only the *height* of the log-likelihood,
gives the *same* answer under any monotone reparameterization (the height of the peak does
not move when you relabel the axis). This non-invariance is a genuine defect of Wald, not a
curiosity, and it is the deepest reason to prefer LR when the two disagree.
:::

## The score (Rao) test {#sec-score}

The score test is imported fact 1 read as a test. The key move that distinguishes it from
Wald: it never fits the full model. It asks, *if the null were true, would the
log-likelihood still be trying to climb away from $\theta_0$?* If the data fit the null
well, the score $U_n(\theta_0)$ — the slope at $\theta_0$ — should be near zero (the null
value is near the peak). A steep slope at $\theta_0$ is evidence against the null.

Under the null, $\theta_0$ *is* the truth, so imported fact 1 applies directly:
$\frac1{\sqrt n}U_n(\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0))$. Standardize by
dividing by the standard deviation $\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}$:
$$
\frac{\frac1{\sqrt n}U_n(\theta_0)}{\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}}
= \frac{U_n(\theta_0)}{\sqrt{n I_1(\theta_0)}}
= \frac{U_n(\theta_0)}{\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}}\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \mathcal N(0,1).
$$
Square it:

::: {#thm-score}
**Theorem (score / Rao test).** Under $H_0:\theta=\theta_0$ and the regularity conditions,
$$
\Lambda_{\mathrm S} = \frac{U_n(\theta_0)^2}{I_n(\theta_0)}\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \chi^2_1 .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Under the null $\theta_0$ is the true parameter, so imported fact 1 gives
$\frac1{\sqrt n}U_n(\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0))$. Dividing this
$\mathcal N(0,I_1(\theta_0))$ variable by the constant $\sqrt{I_1(\theta_0)}$ scales its
variance by $1/I_1(\theta_0)$, giving $\mathcal N(0,1)$; the left side equals
$U_n(\theta_0)/\sqrt{n I_1(\theta_0)}=U_n(\theta_0)/\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}$. By the imported
chi-square fact, the square of this standard Normal converges to $\chi^2_1$, and that square
is exactly $\Lambda_{\mathrm S}=U_n(\theta_0)^2/I_n(\theta_0)$. Note the crucial difference
from @thm-wald: here the information is evaluated at the *known* null value $\theta_0$, not
at $\hat\theta_n$ — so **no full-model fit is required**, and there is no Slutsky
substitution because $I_n(\theta_0)$ is already a known constant under $H_0$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The score test's selling point is the mirror image of Wald's: **it uses only the null
fit.** In the scalar simple-null case you fit nothing at all — $\theta_0$ is given, and you
just evaluate the slope and the information there. In the realistic case with nuisance
parameters (test $\psi=\psi_0$ with $\lambda$ free), you fit the *restricted* model — fix
$\psi=\psi_0$, maximize over $\lambda$ to get $\tilde\lambda$ — which is typically far
cheaper than the full model. This is why the score test is the natural **model-building
diagnostic**: it asks "is it worth adding this term?" *without* adding it. In econometrics
it is called the **Lagrange-multiplier test** for exactly this reason — the score at the
restricted optimum is the Lagrange multiplier on the constraint $\psi=\psi_0$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why would I ever use the score test instead of just fitting the full model and using
Wald?"* Three honest reasons. **Cost:** the full model may be expensive or unstable to fit
(many parameters, separation in logistic regression, a hard optimization), while the
restricted model is simple — the score test gets you a test without paying that cost.
**Diagnostics:** the score test is built for "should I add this?" questions — testing
whether to include an omitted variable, a nonlinearity, or autocorrelation — where you
genuinely have not fit the larger model and may not want to. **Boundary behavior:** because
the score test evaluates everything at $\theta_0$ and never relies on $\hat\theta$ being
interior, it behaves better than Wald when the alternative would push the estimate toward a
boundary. The classic application — the **Breusch–Pagan** test for heteroskedasticity and
the **Durbin–Watson**-style tests for autocorrelation in regression — are score tests,
precisely because you want to test the simple model without fitting the elaborate one.
:::

## The likelihood-ratio test and Wilks' theorem {#sec-lr}

The likelihood-ratio statistic is the most direct expression of the question: *how much
better does the free model explain the data than the null-constrained one?* The ratio of
maximized likelihoods,
$$
\Lambda = \frac{\sup_{\theta}L_n(\theta)}{L_n(\theta_0)}
= \frac{L_n(\hat\theta)}{L_n(\theta_0)}\ \ge\ 1,
$$
is at least one (the unconstrained max is at least the constrained value), and large $\Lambda$
means the data prefer the free model. Taking twice the log turns the ratio into a difference
of log-likelihoods and gives the statistic we use:
$$
\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}} = 2\log\Lambda = 2\bigl[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)\bigr]\ \ge\ 0 .
$$ {#eq-LR-def}
The "$2$" is not cosmetic — it is exactly the factor that makes the chi-square come out, as
the derivation now shows. This is **Wilks' theorem** (1938), the keystone of the whole
movement.

::: {#thm-wilks}
**Theorem (Wilks).** Under $H_0:\theta=\theta_0$ and the regularity conditions, for a scalar
$\theta$,
$$
\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}} = 2\bigl[\ell_n(\hat\theta_n)-\ell_n(\theta_0)\bigr]\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \chi^2_1 .
$$
More generally, if $H_0$ imposes $q$ functionally independent constraints on a
$p$-dimensional parameter (leaving $p-q$ free), then $\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}\xrightarrow{d}
\chi^2_q$, where $\hat\theta$ is the unconstrained MLE and $\tilde\theta$ the MLE under
$H_0$, and $\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}=2[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\tilde\theta)]$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Scalar case first.** Start from the quadratic expansion @eq-LRquad derived in
@sec-quadratic:
$$
\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}} = 2\bigl[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)\bigr]
\ \approx\ J_n(\hat\theta)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 ,
$$
where $J_n(\hat\theta)=-\ell_n''(\hat\theta)$ is the observed information. Now rewrite the
right side to expose a standard Normal. Multiply and divide by $I_n(\theta_0)$ — legitimate
since it is positive — and group:
$$
J_n(\hat\theta)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2
= \underbrace{\frac{J_n(\hat\theta)}{I_n(\theta_0)}}_{(\mathrm a)}
\cdot \underbrace{I_n(\theta_0)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2}_{(\mathrm b)} .
$$
Take the two factors in turn. Factor $(\mathrm a)$: by imported fact 3 (and $I_n=nI_1$),
both $\frac1n J_n(\hat\theta)$ and $\frac1n I_n(\theta_0)=I_1(\theta_0)$ converge to
$I_1(\theta_0)$, so their ratio $(\mathrm a)\xrightarrow{P}1$ — it is asymptotically
negligible, a constant $1$. Factor $(\mathrm b)$: write it as the square of a standardized
estimator,
$$
I_n(\theta_0)\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 = \Bigl(\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}\,(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\Bigr)^2 ,
$$
and by imported fact 2 (exactly as in @thm-wald), $\sqrt{I_n(\theta_0)}(\hat\theta-\theta_0)
\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$ under the null. So $(\mathrm b)$ is the square of an
asymptotically standard Normal, hence $(\mathrm b)\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_1$ by the imported
chi-square fact. By **Slutsky's theorem**, a quantity converging in probability to $1$
times a quantity converging in distribution to $\chi^2_1$ converges in distribution to
$\chi^2_1$:
$$
\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}\ \approx\ (\mathrm a)\cdot(\mathrm b)\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ 1\cdot\chi^2_1 = \chi^2_1 .
$$
(The "$\approx$" carries an error term from the third-order Taylor remainder; under the
smoothness condition it is $o_P(1)$ — vanishing in probability — so it does not affect the
limit, which is the standard regularity bookkeeping of [INFR-B4].) This is the scalar claim.

**The $q$-constraint case.** The same expansion runs in $p$ dimensions with the gradient
$\nabla\ell_n$ and the information *matrix* $\mathbf I_n$. The unconstrained MLE $\hat\theta$
and the constrained MLE $\tilde\theta$ each admit a quadratic expansion about the truth; the
difference $2[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\tilde\theta)]$ becomes, after the algebra, a
quadratic form $\mathbf Z^\top \mathbf Z$ in a $q$-dimensional asymptotically standard
Normal vector $\mathbf Z$ — one standard Normal coordinate for each of the $q$ directions
the null pins down, the $p-q$ free directions contributing nothing because the constrained
and unconstrained fits agree there. A sum of $q$ independent squared standard Normals is
$\chi^2_q$ by the imported chi-square fact. The geometric content — *the degrees of freedom
count the dimensions the null removes* — is the part worth carrying; the matrix algebra is
the multivariate echo of the scalar lines above. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The degrees-of-freedom rule deserves its own sentence because it is the single most
error-prone part of applying these tests: **the chi-square degrees of freedom equal the
number of free parameters the null fixes — equivalently, (parameters in the big model) −
(parameters in the small model).** Testing one coefficient is zero: $q=1$. Testing whether
three coefficients are jointly zero: $q=3$. Testing whether two nested models differ, where
the big model has five parameters and the small one three: $q=2$. Get $q$ wrong and the
p-value is meaningless even though the statistic is correct.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does the chi-square come from — why that distribution and not some other?"* Trace
it to its source: the MLE error $\hat\theta-\theta_0$ is asymptotically **Gaussian**
([INFR-B4]), the test statistics are all (to leading order) the **squared** standardized
version of that error, and *the square of a standard Normal is $\chi^2_1$* — sum $q$ of them
for $q$ constraints and you get $\chi^2_q$. The chi-square is not a new assumption; it is
the Gaussian asymptotics of the MLE, squared. If a follow-up asks why the *F*-distribution
appears instead in linear regression, the answer is that for the exact Normal-linear model
with *unknown variance* the relevant statistic is a ratio of two chi-squares (numerator and
estimated-variance denominator), which is an $F$; the LR/Wald/score chi-squares are the
large-sample version where the variance is known well enough that the denominator's
randomness vanishes and $F\cdot q\to\chi^2_q$. [INFR-D2] derived that $F$; this article is
its asymptotic, distribution-free cousin.
:::

## The deviance: Wilks in the language Regression speaks {#sec-deviance}

A vocabulary note that pays off immediately in the Regression track. For a fitted model,
the **deviance** is defined as
$$
D = 2\bigl[\ell_n(\text{saturated})-\ell_n(\hat\theta)\bigr],
$$
twice the gap between the log-likelihood of a "saturated" model (one parameter per
observation, fitting the data perfectly) and the fitted model. The saturated term is a
*constant* — it does not depend on which nested model you fit — so the **difference of
deviances** between a small model and a big model is exactly the likelihood-ratio statistic:
$$
D_{\text{small}}-D_{\text{big}}
= 2\bigl[\ell_n(\text{big})-\ell_n(\text{small})\bigr]
= \Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}\ \xrightarrow{\ d\ }\ \chi^2_{\,q},
$$
the saturated constant cancelling. So "compare two nested GLMs by the change in deviance,
referred to a chi-square with degrees of freedom equal to the difference in parameters" is
*literally* Wilks' theorem in disguise. When [REGR] tests whether a logistic or Poisson
regression term improves the fit, it is computing $\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}$ and reading
@thm-wilks. This article is where that test is justified.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run all three tests on real, messy problems — never a toy rigged to flatter them.
The first we carry through by hand, number by number, so the formulas breathe; the last
sits squarely on the edge where they part company.

## Example 1 — a binomial proportion, all three by hand {#sec-ex-binomial}

A quality-control engineer inspects $n=200$ welds and finds $k=18$ defective. The standard
assumes a defect rate of $\theta_0=0.05$; the data suggest higher. Test $H_0:\theta=0.05$
against $H_1:\theta\ne0.05$ three ways. This is the cleanest possible demonstration because
every piece has a closed form ([INFR-B2], [INFR-B3] gave them): the MLE is $\hat\theta=k/n$,
the log-likelihood is $\ell_n(\theta)=k\log\theta+(n-k)\log(1-\theta)$ plus a constant, the
score is $U_n(\theta)=\frac{k}{\theta}-\frac{n-k}{1-\theta}$, and the information is
$I_n(\theta)=\frac{n}{\theta(1-\theta)}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-binomial-trinity
#| fig-cap: 'All three tests on one binomial log-likelihood ($n=200$, $k=18$, $H_0:\theta_0=0.05$). The black curve is $\ell_n(\theta)$; the green dot is the MLE $\hat\theta=0.09$; the red tick is $\theta_0=0.05$. **Wald** (green) reads the horizontal gap scaled by the curvature *at the peak*; **score** (orange) reads the slope *at $\theta_0$*; **likelihood-ratio** (purple) reads the vertical drop. The three statistics, printed below, are close but not equal — the mild skew of the binomial log-likelihood at this $n$ separates them, exactly the higher-order terms the parabola dropped.'
n, k = 200, 18
theta0 = 0.05
thhat = k/n

def loglik(t):  # up to an additive constant
    return k*np.log(t) + (n-k)*np.log(1-t)

def score(t):
    return k/t - (n-k)/(1-t)

def info(t):    # total Fisher information I_n(theta) = n / [theta(1-theta)]
    return n/(t*(1-t))

# the three statistics
lam_W  = (thhat-theta0)**2 * info(thhat)
lam_S  = score(theta0)**2 / info(theta0)
lam_LR = 2*(loglik(thhat) - loglik(theta0))

t = np.linspace(0.01, 0.20, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.4))
ax.plot(t, loglik(t), color="black", lw=2.2, label=r"$\ell_n(\theta)$")
ax.scatter([thhat],[loglik(thhat)], color=ACCENT, s=60, zorder=6)
ax.axvline(thhat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, ls=":"); ax.text(thhat+0.004, loglik(t).min()+1, r"$\hat\theta$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
ax.axvline(theta0, color=CURVE, lw=1.0, ls=":"); ax.text(theta0-0.012, loglik(t).min()+1, r"$\theta_0$", color=CURVE, fontsize=12)
# vertical LR drop
ax.annotate("", xy=(theta0, loglik(theta0)), xytext=(theta0, loglik(thhat)),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=LR, lw=2.0))
# score tangent at theta0
sl = score(theta0)
tx = np.linspace(theta0-0.015, theta0+0.02, 40)
ax.plot(tx, loglik(theta0)+sl*(tx-theta0), color=SCORE, lw=2.0, ls="--", label="slope at $\\theta_0$ (score)")
# wald parabola at the peak
par = loglik(thhat) - 0.5*info(thhat)*(t-thhat)**2
ax.plot(t, par, color=WALD, lw=1.6, ls="-.", alpha=0.8, label="parabola at peak (Wald)")
ax.set_ylim(loglik(t).min()-1, loglik(thhat)+2)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\theta$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\ell_n(\theta)$ (up to a constant)")
ax.set_title("Three readings of the binomial log-likelihood")
ax.legend(loc="lower center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

from math import erf, sqrt
def chi2_1_sf(x):  # P(chi^2_1 > x) = 2*(1 - Phi(sqrt(x)))
    return 2*(1 - 0.5*(1+erf(sqrt(x)/sqrt(2))))
print(f"MLE theta_hat = {thhat:.4f},  null theta0 = {theta0}")
print(f"Wald   statistic = {lam_W:.4f},  p = {chi2_1_sf(lam_W):.4f}")
print(f"Score  statistic = {lam_S:.4f},  p = {chi2_1_sf(lam_S):.4f}")
print(f"LR     statistic = {lam_LR:.4f},  p = {chi2_1_sf(lam_LR):.4f}")
```

Walk the arithmetic the code computed, plugging into the boxed formulas.

- The MLE is $\hat\theta=k/n=18/200=`{python} f"{thhat:.3f}"`$ — the defect rate is nearly
  double the standard's $0.05$.
- **Wald.** The information at the peak is $I_n(\hat\theta)=n/[\hat\theta(1-\hat\theta)]$.
  The statistic is $(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2 I_n(\hat\theta)=`{python} f"{lam_W:.3f}"`$, i.e.
  the squared standardized distance of $0.09$ from $0.05$.
- **Score.** The slope at the null is $U_n(\theta_0)=\frac{k}{\theta_0}-\frac{n-k}{1-\theta_0}$
  and the information there is $I_n(\theta_0)=n/[\theta_0(1-\theta_0)]$; the statistic is
  $U_n(\theta_0)^2/I_n(\theta_0)=`{python} f"{lam_S:.3f}"`$ — no full-model fit was needed,
  only evaluation at $\theta_0=0.05$.
- **Likelihood-ratio.** Twice the height drop, $2[\ell_n(\hat\theta)-\ell_n(\theta_0)]
  =`{python} f"{lam_LR:.3f}"`$.

All three exceed the $\chi^2_1$ critical value $3.84$ (the $0.95$ quantile), so all three
reject $H_0$ at the $5\%$ level — the defect rate is genuinely elevated. But notice they are
*not identical*: $`{python} f"{lam_W:.2f}"`$ vs. $`{python} f"{lam_S:.2f}"`$ vs.
$`{python} f"{lam_LR:.2f}"`$. That spread is the higher-order skew of the binomial
log-likelihood at $n=200$ — small here, but the seed of the disaster in @sec-failure when
$n$ is small and $\theta_0$ is near the boundary.

## Example 2 — a Poisson rate and the duality with the confidence interval {#sec-ex-poisson}

A call center logs $n=30$ days; the total calls are $\sum x_i = 246$, so the daily rate MLE
is $\hat\lambda=\bar x=8.2$. Management's staffing model assumes $\lambda_0=7$. Test
$H_0:\lambda=7$. For the Poisson ([INFR-B2], [INFR-B3]): $\ell_n(\lambda)=
(\sum x_i)\log\lambda - n\lambda$ plus a constant, $U_n(\lambda)=\frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda}-n$,
$I_n(\lambda)=n/\lambda$. We use this example to make the **test/interval duality** of
[INFR-D1] and [INFR-C3] concrete: the set of null values a level-$\alpha$ test does *not*
reject is exactly the $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval.

```{python}
#| label: fig-poisson-duality
#| fig-cap: 'Test/interval duality for a Poisson rate ($n=30$, $\sum x_i=246$, $\hat\lambda=8.2$). For each candidate null $\lambda_0$ on the horizontal axis we compute the Wald (green), score (orange), and LR (purple) statistics for $H_0:\lambda=\lambda_0$ and plot them against the $\chi^2_1$ critical value $3.84$ (grey). The set of $\lambda_0$ whose statistic falls *below* the line — the values a 5% test fails to reject — is the 95% confidence interval for that method (shown as the horizontal bars). The LR and score curves are asymmetric about $\hat\lambda$ (they follow the curve), so their intervals are asymmetric; the Wald curve is an exact parabola, so its interval is symmetric. The three intervals are the confidence-set duals of [INFR-C3].'
n = 30; S = 246
lamhat = S/n
def ell_p(l): return S*np.log(l) - n*l
def score_p(l): return S/l - n
def info_p(l): return n/l

l0 = np.linspace(5.5, 11.2, 500)
W  = (lamhat-l0)**2 * info_p(lamhat)
Sc = score_p(l0)**2 / info_p(l0)
LRc= 2*(ell_p(lamhat) - ell_p(l0))
crit = 3.841

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.4))
ax.plot(l0, W,  color=WALD,  label="Wald")
ax.plot(l0, Sc, color=SCORE, label="score")
ax.plot(l0, LRc,color=LR,    label="likelihood-ratio")
ax.axhline(crit, color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--", label=r"$\chi^2_{1,0.95}=3.84$")
ax.axvline(lamhat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, ls=":"); ax.text(lamhat+0.05, 9.0, r"$\hat\lambda$", color=ACCENT)

# extract intervals (where each curve <= crit) and draw as bars near the bottom
def interval(curve):
    below = l0[curve <= crit]
    return below.min(), below.max()
for curve, col, yy, name in [(W, WALD, -0.8, "Wald"), (Sc, SCORE, -1.6, "score"), (LRc, LR, -2.4, "LR")]:
    lo, hi = interval(curve)
    ax.plot([lo, hi], [yy, yy], color=col, lw=4, solid_capstyle="round")
    ax.text(hi+0.08, yy, f"{name}: [{lo:.2f}, {hi:.2f}]", color=col, fontsize=8.5, va="center")
ax.set_ylim(-3.0, 10)
ax.set_xlabel(r"null value $\lambda_0$"); ax.set_ylabel("test statistic")
ax.set_title("Acceptance region = confidence interval (test/interval duality)")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9, ncol=2)
plt.tight_layout()

from math import erf, sqrt
def chi2_1_sf(x): return 2*(1 - 0.5*(1+erf(sqrt(x)/sqrt(2))))
W0  = (lamhat-7)**2*info_p(lamhat)
S0  = score_p(7.0)**2/info_p(7.0)
LR0 = 2*(ell_p(lamhat)-ell_p(7.0))
print(f"Testing H0: lambda = 7  (lambda_hat = {lamhat:.3f})")
print(f"Wald : {W0:.3f}  p = {chi2_1_sf(W0):.4f}")
print(f"Score: {S0:.3f}  p = {chi2_1_sf(S0):.4f}")
print(f"LR   : {LR0:.3f}  p = {chi2_1_sf(LR0):.4f}")
```

Reading the output: testing $H_0:\lambda=7$, all three statistics are well above $3.84$ and
all three p-values are small — the rate is significantly above $7$. The figure makes the
deeper point. **The confidence interval is the test run at every null value at once.** The
horizontal bars are the sets of $\lambda_0$ the $5\%$ test does not reject, and they are
*exactly* the Wald, score, and LR confidence intervals of [INFR-C3] — the test and the
interval are two views of the same object, the duality [INFR-D1] proved. Notice the Wald
interval is symmetric about $\hat\lambda$ (its statistic is an exact parabola in
$\lambda_0$) while the score and LR intervals lean — they follow the asymmetric likelihood —
which is precisely why, near a boundary, they behave better.

## Example 3 — a joint test with nuisance parameters: logistic regression {#sec-ex-logistic}

The realistic case: a model with several parameters, where we test a *subset*. This is where
$q>1$ degrees of freedom appear and where the LR/deviance machinery earns its keep. We
simulate a logistic regression — a binary outcome (say, customer churn) driven by two
predictors plus an intercept — and test whether the *two slopes are jointly zero*
($H_0:\beta_1=\beta_2=0$, $q=2$ constraints, the intercept a free nuisance parameter under
both hypotheses). There is no closed form; we fit by Newton's method (the [INFR-B2]
machinery) and compare the maximized log-likelihoods.

```{python}
#| label: fig-logistic-joint
#| fig-cap: 'A joint likelihood-ratio test in logistic regression ($n=400$, two genuine predictors). We fit the full model (intercept + two slopes) and the null model (intercept only), then compute the deviance drop $\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}=2[\ell(\text{full})-\ell(\text{null})]$ and refer it to $\chi^2_2$. **Left:** the fitted churn probability vs. the first predictor, full model (curve) over the binary data (jittered). **Right:** the $\chi^2_2$ reference density (red) with the observed LR statistic marked (purple) — far into the tail, so the two slopes are jointly highly significant. The degrees of freedom are $2$ because the null fixes two parameters.'
def sigmoid(z): return 1/(1+np.exp(-z))

n = 400
X1 = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
X2 = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
beta_true = np.array([-0.4, 1.1, -0.8])   # intercept, slope1, slope2
Xfull = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), X1, X2])
p_true = sigmoid(Xfull @ beta_true)
y = rng.binomial(1, p_true)

def fit_logistic(Xmat, y, iters=50):
    """Newton-Raphson MLE for logistic regression (the INFR-B2 optimizer)."""
    b = np.zeros(Xmat.shape[1])
    for _ in range(iters):
        eta = Xmat @ b
        p = sigmoid(eta)
        W = p*(1-p)
        grad = Xmat.T @ (y - p)
        H = Xmat.T @ (Xmat * W[:, None])           # = -ell'' (observed information)
        b = b + np.linalg.solve(H + 1e-9*np.eye(Xmat.shape[1]), grad)
    return b

def loglik_logistic(Xmat, b, y):
    eta = Xmat @ b
    return np.sum(y*eta - np.log1p(np.exp(eta)))

# full and null (intercept-only) fits
b_full = fit_logistic(Xfull, y)
ll_full = loglik_logistic(Xfull, b_full, y)
Xnull = np.ones((n, 1))
b_null = fit_logistic(Xnull, y)
ll_null = loglik_logistic(Xnull, b_null, y)

LR_joint = 2*(ll_full - ll_null)   # ~ chi^2_2 under H0: beta1 = beta2 = 0

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.9))
order = np.argsort(X1)
axL.scatter(X1, y + rng.uniform(-0.04, 0.04, n), s=12, color=DATA, alpha=0.5, label="data (jittered)")
# fitted prob along X1 at the mean of X2
grid1 = np.linspace(X1.min(), X1.max(), 100)
Xg = np.column_stack([np.ones(100), grid1, np.full(100, X2.mean())])
axL.plot(grid1, sigmoid(Xg @ b_full), color=CURVE, label="fitted $P(y=1)$")
axL.set_xlabel(r"predictor $x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("churn / probability")
axL.set_title("Full logistic fit"); axL.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

# chi^2_2 density: for 2 df it is (1/2) exp(-x/2)
xx = np.linspace(0, max(15, LR_joint*1.1), 300)
chi2_2 = 0.5*np.exp(-xx/2)
axR.plot(xx, chi2_2, color=CURVE, label=r"$\chi^2_2$ density")
axR.axvline(LR_joint, color=LR, lw=2.2, label=fr"observed $\Lambda_{{LR}}={LR_joint:.1f}$")
axR.fill_between(xx[xx>=5.991], chi2_2[xx>=5.991], color=FILL, alpha=0.5)
axR.axvline(5.991, color=GREY, lw=1.2, ls="--", label=r"$\chi^2_{2,0.95}=5.99$")
axR.set_xlabel("statistic"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title("LR statistic vs. its $\\chi^2_2$ null"); axR.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

from math import exp
def chi2_2_sf(x): return exp(-x/2)   # survival function of chi^2_2
print(f"full-model log-likelihood = {ll_full:.3f}  (beta_hat = {np.round(b_full,3)})")
print(f"null-model log-likelihood = {ll_null:.3f}")
print(f"LR statistic = 2*(ll_full - ll_null) = {LR_joint:.3f}")
print(f"degrees of freedom q = 2,  p-value = {chi2_2_sf(LR_joint):.2e}")
```

The full model recovers slopes near the true $(1.1,-0.8)$; the deviance drop from the
intercept-only model is $\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}=`{python} f"{LR_joint:.1f}"`$, referred to
$\chi^2_2$ (two slopes fixed under the null) for a p-value of
$`{python} f"{chi2_2_sf(LR_joint):.0e}"`$. The two predictors jointly carry real signal.
Three lessons this example makes concrete:

- **This is the test every GLM report runs.** "Model chi-square," "likelihood-ratio test
  vs. null," "drop-in-deviance test" — all are $\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}$ with the appropriate
  $q$. The Regression track consumes exactly this.
- **The degrees of freedom are the count of fixed parameters**, here $q=2$. Had we tested
  only $\beta_1=0$ with $\beta_2$ free, the null model would refit with $\beta_2$ included
  and $q=1$.
- **A Wald or score version exists too**, using the information *matrix* and the quadratic
  form of @thm-wilks. Software usually reports the Wald $z$ for each coefficient (cheap, full
  fit only) and the LR for the joint or nested comparison (more reliable). Same trinity,
  vector form.

## Example 4 — the edge of applicability: where Wald breaks and the trinity splits {#sec-failure}

Now the honest boundary, and the most important example in the article. The three tests are
equal *asymptotically*; for small $n$ and parameters near a boundary they can disagree
violently, and Wald is the one that fails — sometimes catastrophically. We make this
visible with a binomial proportion at small $n$ near the floor, the same regime that broke
the Wald interval in [INFR-B4] and [INFR-C3].

Suppose $n=25$ trials yield $k=1$ success, so $\hat\theta=0.04$, and we test $H_0:\theta=0.20$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-trinity-disagree
#| fig-cap: 'The trinity comes apart at small $n$ near the boundary ($n=25$, $k=1$, $H_0:\theta_0=0.20$). The binomial log-likelihood (black) is sharply *right-skewed* about the tiny MLE $\hat\theta=0.04$: very steeply curved at the peak, then a long gentle climb out toward $\theta_0$. **Wald** fits a symmetric parabola using the curvature *at the peak* (green dashed) — which is far too steep to describe the gentle stretch out to $\theta_0$ — so it badly *overstates* the distance and returns the largest statistic. **Score** (orange tangent at $\theta_0$) uses the gentle curvature at the null and returns the smallest; **likelihood-ratio** (purple drop) reads the honest height and sits between. The printed statistics disagree by a factor of four; the LR is the trustworthy one.'
n, k = 25, 1
theta0 = 0.20
thhat = k/n
def ll_b(t): return k*np.log(t) + (n-k)*np.log(1-t)
def sc_b(t): return k/t - (n-k)/(1-t)
def in_b(t): return n/(t*(1-t))

lam_W  = (thhat-theta0)**2 * in_b(thhat)
lam_S  = sc_b(theta0)**2 / in_b(theta0)
lam_LR = 2*(ll_b(thhat) - ll_b(theta0))

t = np.linspace(0.005, 0.45, 500)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.4))
ax.plot(t, ll_b(t), color="black", lw=2.2, label=r"$\ell_n(\theta)$ (right-skewed)")
ax.scatter([thhat],[ll_b(thhat)], color=ACCENT, s=60, zorder=6)
ax.axvline(thhat, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, ls=":"); ax.text(thhat+0.006, ll_b(t).min()+0.5, r"$\hat\theta$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
ax.axvline(theta0, color=CURVE, lw=1.0, ls=":"); ax.text(theta0+0.006, ll_b(t).min()+0.5, r"$\theta_0$", color=CURVE, fontsize=12)
# wald parabola
par = ll_b(thhat) - 0.5*in_b(thhat)*(t-thhat)**2
ax.plot(t, par, color=WALD, lw=1.6, ls="--", label="Wald parabola at peak")
# score tangent at theta0
sl = sc_b(theta0)
tx = np.linspace(theta0-0.05, theta0+0.06, 40)
ax.plot(tx, ll_b(theta0)+sl*(tx-theta0), color=SCORE, lw=2.0, ls="-.", label="score slope at $\\theta_0$")
# LR drop
ax.annotate("", xy=(theta0, ll_b(theta0)), xytext=(theta0, ll_b(thhat)),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=LR, lw=2.0))
ax.set_ylim(ll_b(t).min()-0.5, ll_b(thhat)+1.5)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\theta$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\ell_n(\theta)$ (up to a constant)")
ax.set_title("Small $n$, near boundary: Wald's parabola is the wrong shape")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

from math import erf, sqrt
def chi2_1_sf(x): return 2*(1 - 0.5*(1+erf(sqrt(x)/sqrt(2))))
print(f"n={n}, k={k}, theta_hat={thhat:.3f}, testing H0: theta={theta0}")
print(f"Wald  statistic = {lam_W:6.3f}   p = {chi2_1_sf(lam_W):.4f}   {'REJECT' if lam_W>3.841 else 'fail to reject'}")
print(f"Score statistic = {lam_S:6.3f}   p = {chi2_1_sf(lam_S):.4f}   {'REJECT' if lam_S>3.841 else 'fail to reject'}")
print(f"LR    statistic = {lam_LR:6.3f}   p = {chi2_1_sf(lam_LR):.4f}   {'REJECT' if lam_LR>3.841 else 'fail to reject'}")
```

The numbers tell the story the picture set up — and they disagree by a factor of four
($\Lambda_{\mathrm W}\approx16.7$, $\Lambda_{\mathrm{LR}}\approx5.5$,
$\Lambda_{\mathrm S}\approx4.0$). With $\hat\theta=0.04$ jammed against the floor, the
curvature *at the peak* is enormous — the information $I_n(\hat\theta)=n/[\hat\theta
(1-\hat\theta)]$ blows up as $\hat\theta\to0$ — so Wald's parabola is far too steep to
describe the long, gentle climb out to $\theta_0=0.20$. It therefore *overstates* the
distance and returns by far the largest statistic, the most aggressive rejection. The score
statistic uses the much gentler curvature at $\theta_0$ and returns the smallest; the LR
reads the honest height drop and lands between. Here all three happen to reject, but the
fourfold spread is the warning: **at this $n$ the three are no longer interchangeable, and
Wald — whose value depends entirely on a peak curvature that misrepresents the rest of the
curve — is the one to distrust.** In a less extreme case the same mechanism makes Wald reject
when LR would not, or (the Hauck–Donner symptom below) the reverse.

This is not a contrived corner; it is the everyday hazard of small counts, rare events, and
coefficients near the edge of their range. Three consolidated lessons:

- **Wald is the fragile corner.** It relies entirely on the curvature *at the peak* being a
  good guide to the curvature *everywhere between $\hat\theta$ and $\theta_0$*. When the
  likelihood is skewed (small $n$, near-boundary parameter, a wild reparameterization) that
  assumption fails and Wald is miscalibrated — and, by the non-invariance pitfall of
  @sec-wald, even the *direction* of the error depends on the parameterization.
- **The Hauck–Donner effect is the extreme case.** In logistic regression, as a
  coefficient's true value grows, the Wald statistic can *decrease* toward zero — the test
  becomes *less* likely to reject the larger the effect, because the standard error inflates
  faster than the estimate. The LR test does not suffer this. A Wald $z$ near zero is not
  always evidence of no effect; it can be evidence of a *huge* one the test cannot see.
- **Default to LR (or score) when the likelihood is skewed.** The likelihood-ratio test is
  invariant to reparameterization and uses the curve's true shape; the score test is the
  cheap, boundary-respecting alternative when you do not want to fit the full model. Reserve
  Wald for the regime it is good in: large $n$, interior parameter, roughly symmetric
  likelihood — exactly when all three agree anyway.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**"All three are asymptotically equivalent" is not "all three give the same answer."** The
equivalence is a $n\to\infty$ theorem. When a Wald, score, and LR test of the *same*
hypothesis disagree on your data, that disagreement is *information*: it means the
log-likelihood is not a symmetric parabola at your sample size, the chi-square approximation
is strained, and you should (a) trust the LR test, (b) consider a profile-likelihood or
exact method, or (c) get more data. Quoting whichever of the three crossed your significance
threshold is p-hacking by another name.
:::

# Why the likelihood ratio is special: the Neyman–Pearson lemma {#sec-np}

The trinity puts the likelihood ratio at its center, and Wilks' theorem makes it the most
trustworthy of the three. But there is a *deeper* reason likelihood ratios are the right
object — one that predates the asymptotics and justifies them. For the cleanest possible
testing problem, **the likelihood-ratio test is provably the best test that exists**. This
is the **Neyman–Pearson lemma** (1933), the theoretical foundation of the entire enterprise.

## The setup: simple vs. simple

Consider the simplest hypothesis-testing problem: both hypotheses are **simple**, meaning
each fully specifies the distribution. We have data $\mathbf x$ with density $f_0(\mathbf
x)$ under $H_0$ and $f_1(\mathbf x)$ under $H_1$ — two completely specified competing
densities, no free parameters on either side:
$$
H_0:\ \mathbf X\sim f_0
\qquad\text{vs.}\qquad
H_1:\ \mathbf X\sim f_1 .
$$
A test is a decision rule: a region $R$ in data space such that we reject $H_0$ when
$\mathbf x\in R$. Its **size** (Type I error, [INFR-D1]) is $\alpha=P_0(\mathbf X\in R)=
\int_R f_0$, the chance of rejecting when $H_0$ is true. Its **power** (the chance of
correctly rejecting when $H_1$ is true) is $1-\beta=P_1(\mathbf X\in R)=\int_R f_1$. Among
all tests of a fixed size $\alpha$, we want the one with the **largest power** — the most
sensitive detector that still keeps false alarms at $\alpha$.

The lemma says the answer is the **likelihood-ratio test**: reject $H_0$ when the ratio of
likelihoods is large,
$$
R^\star = \Bigl\{\mathbf x:\ \frac{f_1(\mathbf x)}{f_0(\mathbf x)}\ge k\Bigr\},
$$ {#eq-np-region}
with the threshold $k$ chosen so the size is exactly $\alpha$. *Rank the data by how much
more likely they are under $H_1$ than $H_0$, and reject the top $\alpha$-fraction.*

::: {#thm-np}
**Theorem (Neyman–Pearson lemma).** For testing the simple $H_0:f_0$ against the simple
$H_1:f_1$, let $R^\star$ be the likelihood-ratio region @eq-np-region with $k$ chosen so
that $\int_{R^\star}f_0 = \alpha$. Then $R^\star$ is **most powerful** at level $\alpha$:
any other test $R$ with size $\int_R f_0\le\alpha$ has power $\int_R f_1\le\int_{R^\star}f_1$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The proof is one clean inequality; we show every step. Let $R$ be any competing test with
size $\le\alpha$. Encode each region by its **indicator function** — write
$\mathbf 1_{R^\star}(\mathbf x)=1$ if $\mathbf x\in R^\star$ and $0$ otherwise, similarly
$\mathbf 1_{R}$. The claim "$R^\star$ has at least as much power as $R$" is the statement
$$
\int \bigl(\mathbf 1_{R^\star}-\mathbf 1_{R}\bigr)\,f_1\ \ge\ 0 .
$$

**The key pointwise inequality.** Consider the function
$$
g(\mathbf x) = \bigl(\mathbf 1_{R^\star}(\mathbf x)-\mathbf 1_{R}(\mathbf x)\bigr)\,
\bigl(f_1(\mathbf x)-k\,f_0(\mathbf x)\bigr) .
$$
We claim $g(\mathbf x)\ge 0$ **everywhere** — check the two cases that matter:

- If $\mathbf x\in R^\star$, then by the definition @eq-np-region, $f_1\ge k f_0$, so the
  second factor $f_1-kf_0\ge 0$. The first factor is $1-\mathbf 1_R\in\{0,1\}\ge 0$. Product
  of two non-negatives: $g\ge 0$.
- If $\mathbf x\notin R^\star$, then $f_1< k f_0$, so the second factor $f_1-kf_0<0$. The
  first factor is $0-\mathbf 1_R\in\{-1,0\}\le 0$. Product of a non-positive and a negative:
  $g\ge 0$.

(When $\mathbf x\in R^\star$ *and* $\mathbf x\in R$, the first factor is $0$ and $g=0$;
likewise outside both. The inequality holds in every case.) So $g\ge 0$ pointwise, hence its
integral is non-negative:
$$
0\ \le\ \int g
= \int\bigl(\mathbf 1_{R^\star}-\mathbf 1_{R}\bigr)f_1
\;-\; k\int\bigl(\mathbf 1_{R^\star}-\mathbf 1_{R}\bigr)f_0 .
$$

**Read the two integrals.** The first is exactly the power difference,
$\int_{R^\star}f_1-\int_R f_1$. The second is $k$ times the size difference,
$\int_{R^\star}f_0-\int_R f_0 = \alpha - \text{size}(R)\ge 0$ (since $R^\star$ has size
exactly $\alpha$ and $R$ has size at most $\alpha$). Rearranging the inequality,
$$
\underbrace{\int_{R^\star}f_1-\int_R f_1}_{\text{power difference}}
\ \ge\ k\underbrace{\Bigl(\alpha-\text{size}(R)\Bigr)}_{\ge\,0} \ \ge\ 0 ,
$$
where the last step uses $k\ge 0$ (a likelihood ratio threshold is non-negative). So the
power of $R^\star$ is at least the power of $R$. Since $R$ was an arbitrary test of size
$\le\alpha$, $R^\star$ is most powerful. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The proof's engine is worth saying in words because it is the *why* behind the entire
trinity: **to maximize power for a fixed false-alarm budget, spend that budget on the data
points that most discriminate between the two hypotheses — and the likelihood ratio is
exactly the measure of how discriminating each data point is.** Every unit of size $\alpha$
"buys" the most power when allocated to the highest-likelihood-ratio outcomes. The
likelihood ratio is not one option among many; it is *the* optimal currency of evidence.

## From simple-vs-simple to the real world

Neyman–Pearson is about *simple* hypotheses, but real hypotheses are usually **composite** —
$H_1:\theta\ne\theta_0$ is a whole family, not a single density. The lemma does not directly
hand us a most-powerful test there (in general none exists uniformly over the alternative).
But it justifies the likelihood ratio as the right building block, and two bridges connect it
to the trinity:

- **Monotone likelihood ratio.** For one-parameter families with a monotone likelihood ratio
  in the data (the exponential family — Normal, binomial, Poisson, exponential — all qualify),
  the Neyman–Pearson region for *every* simple alternative on one side is the *same* region,
  so the likelihood-ratio test is **uniformly most powerful** for the one-sided composite
  $H_1:\theta>\theta_0$. This is the cleanest extension and covers most one-sided tests in
  practice.
- **The generalized likelihood ratio.** For composite-vs-composite, we replace each density
  by its *maximized* likelihood — $\sup_{\theta\in H_1}L/\sup_{\theta\in H_0}L$ — which is
  exactly the $\Lambda$ of @sec-lr. It is no longer provably optimal in finite samples, but
  it inherits Neyman–Pearson's logic and, by Wilks, a tractable null distribution. **The
  generalized likelihood-ratio test is the practical descendant of the lemma**, and its
  asymptotic chi-square is what makes it usable.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does the Neyman–Pearson lemma actually guarantee, and what doesn't it?"* It
guarantees that for *simple-vs-simple* testing, the likelihood-ratio test is most powerful
at any level — the best possible detector for a fixed false-alarm rate. It does **not**
guarantee a uniformly most powerful test for two-sided or composite alternatives (one
usually does not exist), and it says nothing about *asymptotics* — it is an exact,
finite-sample result. Its role is foundational: it is *why* we center testing on likelihood
ratios in the first place, which Wilks then makes practical by giving the generalized
likelihood ratio a chi-square null distribution. The crisp answer ties them together:
Neyman–Pearson says likelihood ratios are optimal; Wilks says they are computable; together
they are the justification for the LR test being the default.
:::

# When the three disagree, and which to trust {#sec-disagree}

We have met the disagreement empirically (@sec-failure) and structurally (@sec-quadratic).
Here is the consolidated practitioner's map — the judgment a graduate-level command requires.

```{python}
#| label: fig-disagreement-sweep
#| fig-cap: 'How the disagreement grows as the sample shrinks. For a binomial test of $H_0:\theta=0.5$ with a fixed observed proportion $\hat\theta=0.7$, we sweep the sample size $n$ and plot all three statistics. At large $n$ (right) the three curves are indistinguishable — Wilks'' equivalence. As $n$ falls (left) they separate: Wald drifts away from the LR and score, because the quadratic approximation at the peak degrades fastest. The vertical gap between the curves *is* the breakdown of the asymptotic equivalence, made visible.'
theta0 = 0.5
thhat = 0.7
ns = np.unique(np.round(np.logspace(np.log10(8), np.log10(2000), 40)).astype(int))
def ll_b(k,n,t): return k*np.log(t)+(n-k)*np.log(1-t)
W_list, S_list, LR_list = [], [], []
for nn in ns:
    k = thhat*nn
    info_hat = nn/(thhat*(1-thhat))
    info_0   = nn/(theta0*(1-theta0))
    sc0 = k/theta0 - (nn-k)/(1-theta0)
    W_list.append((thhat-theta0)**2*info_hat)
    S_list.append(sc0**2/info_0)
    LR_list.append(2*(ll_b(k,nn,thhat)-ll_b(k,nn,theta0)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.plot(ns, W_list,  color=WALD,  marker="o", ms=3, label="Wald")
ax.plot(ns, S_list,  color=SCORE, marker="s", ms=3, label="score")
ax.plot(ns, LR_list, color=LR,    marker="^", ms=3, label="likelihood-ratio")
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$ (log scale)"); ax.set_ylabel("test statistic (log scale)")
ax.set_title(r"The three agree as $n\to\infty$, split as $n$ shrinks")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The figure is the whole section in one image: identical at large $n$, separating as $n$
falls. The rules to carry:

- **They agree when the log-likelihood is a symmetric parabola** — large $n$, interior
  parameter, no wild reparameterization. In that regime use whichever is cheapest, which is
  usually the **Wald** statistic your software already printed.
- **They disagree when the likelihood is skewed** — small $n$, parameter near a boundary, or
  a heavily nonlinear parameterization. There, **prefer the likelihood-ratio test**: it is
  invariant to reparameterization, uses the curve's true shape, and is the most reliable of
  the three. Its only cost is needing both the constrained and unconstrained fits.
- **Use the score test when you cannot or will not fit the full model** — model-building
  diagnostics ("should I add this term?"), expensive or unstable full fits, and
  boundary-respecting tests. It needs only the restricted fit.
- **Distrust the Wald test specifically when** the estimate is far from the null in
  standard-error units, the parameter is near a boundary, or you observe the Hauck–Donner
  symptom (a large effect with a small Wald statistic). When Wald disagrees with LR, LR is
  almost always right.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You run a Wald test and a likelihood-ratio test on the same coefficient and get different
p-values — which do you report, and why?"* Report the **likelihood-ratio** test, and say why:
it is invariant to how the model is parameterized, it uses the actual shape of the
log-likelihood rather than a parabola fit at the peak, and it is more reliable when the
likelihood is skewed — which is exactly the situation that *caused* the two to disagree in
the first place. The disagreement itself is diagnostic: it tells you the sample is small
enough, or the parameter close enough to a boundary, that the quadratic approximation behind
the Wald test is strained. If you want to go further, mention the profile-likelihood interval
([INFR-C3]) as the interval-side dual, or an exact/bootstrap method ([INFR-E1]) when even the
LR's chi-square approximation is in doubt.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-edge}

The trinity inherits, wholesale, the regularity conditions of [INFR-B4] — because all three
tests are that article's asymptotic normality, squared. Each failure mode below is the
negation of one condition.

- **Small samples (the limit has not arrived).** The chi-square null distribution is a
  large-$n$ approximation. For small $n$ the true null distribution of each statistic differs
  from $\chi^2_q$ — and the three statistics differ from each other (@sec-failure). The
  remedy is an **exact** test where one exists (Fisher's exact test for $2\times2$ tables, the
  exact binomial test), a **profile-likelihood** interval ([INFR-C3]), or a **bootstrap /
  permutation** calibration of the statistic's null distribution ([INFR-E1], [INFR-E3]).
- **Boundary parameters (Wilks' theorem fails).** If the null sits on the boundary of the
  parameter space — testing whether a variance component is *zero*, a mixing proportion is
  $0$ or $1$ — the unconstrained MLE cannot move to one side, the Taylor expansion of
  @sec-quadratic is invalid, and the LR statistic's null distribution is **not** $\chi^2_q$.
  It is typically a *mixture* of chi-squares (Chernoff's result: e.g. a $50\!:\!50$ mix of
  $\chi^2_0$ and $\chi^2_1$ for one boundary parameter), and using the naive $\chi^2_q$
  p-value is conservative-to-wrong. This is the single most common silent error in
  variance-component and mixture testing.
- **Parameter-dependent support.** When the data's support depends on $\theta$ (the uniform
  $[0,\theta]$, [INFR-B2]), the score identities of [INFR-B4] fail, the MLE is not
  $\sqrt n$-normal, and none of the three chi-square results hold. A different,
  model-specific theory is required.
- **Misspecification.** If the model family does not contain the truth, the information
  equality (variance of score = negative expected curvature, [INFR-B4]) breaks, so the Wald
  and score statistics' "information" denominator is the wrong variance and the LR statistic's
  $\chi^2$ is no longer valid. The fixes are the **robust (sandwich) variance** for Wald-type
  tests and, for the LR, **adjusted/robust LR statistics** — the testing analogue of the
  sandwich standard errors of [INFR-B4].

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The chi-square null distribution assumes the null parameter is interior and the model is
correct.** When you test a variance component against zero, a mixture against "no extra
component," or any parameter against its boundary, *do not* use the off-the-shelf $\chi^2_q$
p-value — the asymptotic theory that produced it does not apply, and the true null
distribution is usually a chi-square *mixture* that makes your reported p-value too large
(conservative) or, after naive corrections, too small. Recognizing "my null is on a
boundary" is the trigger to reach for the boundary-corrected distribution or a
simulation-based p-value.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*]** (the null/alternative
structure, the two errors, and the p-value) and **[INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*]** (z, t,
$\chi^2$, F, and the chi-square distribution we consumed). This article generalized all of
them: the trinity tests *any* MLE hypothesis, and Wilks' theorem gives them one null
distribution. It is built directly on **[INFR-B4 *MLE Asymptotics*]** — the three statistics
are that article's asymptotic normality, read three ways and squared — and it is the
test-side companion of **[INFR-C3 *Wald, Score, and Profile Intervals*]**: every test here
inverts to an interval there, the duality made concrete in @sec-ex-poisson. From here:

- **The Regression track** consumes this wholesale. Every nested-model comparison in a GLM
  is the **deviance / LR test** of @sec-deviance; every coefficient $z$-statistic is the
  **Wald test** of @sec-wald; every "should I add this term?" diagnostic (Breusch–Pagan,
  omitted-variable, autocorrelation tests) is a **score / Lagrange-multiplier test**. The
  $F$-test for nested *linear* models in [INFR-D2] is the exact-Normal special case of the
  LR test here.
- **[INFR-D4 *Power and Sample Size*]** asks how *big* an effect each of these tests can
  detect — the power side of the Neyman–Pearson trade-off, made quantitative.
- **[INFR-D5 *Multiple Comparisons*]** asks what happens when you run *many* of these tests
  at once and the false-discovery arithmetic compounds.
- **[INFR-E1 *The Bootstrap*]** and **[INFR-E3 *Permutation Tests*]** are the
  assumption-light alternatives for exactly the small-$n$ and boundary cases where
  @sec-edge says the chi-square approximation cannot be trusted.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding papers.** J. Neyman and E. S. Pearson, "On the Problem of the Most Efficient
  Tests of Statistical Hypotheses," *Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A* 231
  (1933) — the lemma of @sec-np and the birth of the size/power framework. S. S. Wilks, "The
  Large-Sample Distribution of the Likelihood Ratio for Testing Composite Hypotheses,"
  *Annals of Mathematical Statistics* 9 (1938) — @thm-wilks. C. R. Rao, "Large Sample Tests
  of Statistical Hypotheses…," *Proc. Cambridge Phil. Soc.* 44 (1948) — the score test. A.
  Wald, "Tests of Statistical Hypotheses Concerning Several Parameters…," *Trans. AMS* 54
  (1943) — the Wald test.
- **The unifying view.** A. Buse, "The Likelihood Ratio, Wald, and Lagrange Multiplier Tests:
  An Expository Note," *The American Statistician* 36 (1982) — the single best short account
  of the three-readings-of-one-curve geometry this article is built around, with the picture
  of @fig-trinity-geometry.
- **The Wald cautionary tale.** D. Hauck and A. Donner, "Wald's Test as Applied to Hypotheses
  in Logit Analysis," *JASA* 72 (1977) — the effect of @sec-failure, where a larger true
  effect *shrinks* the Wald statistic.
- **Boundary problems.** H. Chernoff, "On the Distribution of the Likelihood Ratio," *Annals
  of Mathematical Statistics* 25 (1954) — the chi-square *mixture* null distribution when the
  parameter is on a boundary, the failure mode of @sec-edge.
- **The standard modern reference.** A. W. van der Vaart, *Asymptotic Statistics* (1998), Ch.
  16, develops all three tests and Wilks' theorem with full rigor and is the definitive source
  for the regularity bookkeeping this article flagged but did not belabor.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** *How much power does each test actually have against
  a given alternative?* — [INFR-D4]. *What if the chi-square approximation is untrustworthy?*
  — bootstrap and permutation calibration, [INFR-E1] and [INFR-E3]. *What does the Bayesian
  do instead of a likelihood-ratio test?* — the **Bayes factor** of [INFR-E4], the ratio of
  *marginal* likelihoods, which integrates over the parameter rather than maximizing — a
  different answer to the same "which model?" question.
